hook: A colossal reclining river-god who, for centuries, was the witty 'voice' that answered back to Pasquino across the city.
facts: A 1st-century AD Roman marble statue of a reclining bearded male deity, usually read as a personification of Ocean or a river-god.
It is one of Rome's six 'talking statues'; from the 16th century anonymous satirical verses (pasquinate) were posted on these statues to mock the powerful without attribution.
Marforio acted as the traditional interlocutor of Pasquino, with the pair staging a mock dialogue of question-and-reply lampoons.
In 1734 it was set into a fountain designed by Filippo Barigioni in the courtyard of the Palazzo Nuovo of the Capitoline Museums, where it remains.
The name is commonly derived from the Latin 'Martis Forum' / 'mare in foro', reflecting its original location near the Forum of Augustus / area of the Mamertine.
legend: Folklore held the talking statues 'conversed' with each other overnight, Marforio asking and Pasquino answering, as Romans woke to find fresh verses pinned to the marble.
w: A colossal reclining river-god in marble who, for three centuries, played straight man to Pasquino. Romans pinned anonymous satires on the city's 'talking statues' to mock the powerful unseen; Marforio asked, Pasquino answered, and the pair staged a running dialogue of lampoons. The story went they conversed by night.

hook: Borromini's giddy spiral lantern twists up like a corkscrew over the courtyard of Rome's old university.
facts: The church was built by Francesco Borromini between 1642 and 1660 within the courtyard of the Palazzo della Sapienza, the seat of Rome's old university.
Its plan is based on a complex interlocking geometry (often described via two overlapping triangles / a star hexagon), carried upward into the dome.
The lantern is crowned by a celebrated helical (corkscrew) spiral, an unprecedented feature in Roman Baroque architecture.
The spiral terminates in a flaming wrought-iron crown topped by a globe, the Pamphilj dove and a cross.
It is dedicated to Saint Yves (Ivo) of Brittany, patron of jurists, fitting its university setting.
legend: The corkscrew lantern is popularly read as echoing the spiral of the Lighthouse of Alexandria or the Tower of Babel, casting the church as a beacon of wisdom for the faithful.
w: Borromini's giddiest flourish: a lantern that twists up like a corkscrew over the courtyard of Rome's old university, the only true spiral in Roman Baroque. The plan below interlocks into a six-pointed star; the spiral climbs to a flaming iron crown and the Pamphilj dove. Some read it as the lighthouse of Alexandria, others as Babel.

hook: Drop a coin in the light box and three Caravaggios flare out of the gloom in the Contarelli Chapel.
facts: The national church of the French in Rome, completed in 1589 to designs associated with Giacomo della Porta and Domenico Fontana.
The Contarelli Chapel holds Caravaggio's three-painting cycle on Saint Matthew, painted 1599-1602: The Calling, The Inspiration (Saint Matthew and the Angel), and The Martyrdom of Saint Matthew.
The contract for the two lateral canvases was signed on 23 July 1599; the cycle was Caravaggio's first major public commission.
The Calling of Saint Matthew is famous for its raking light through which the figure of Christ points toward the tax-collector Matthew.
The chapel was endowed by Cardinal Matteo Contarelli (Matthieu Cointerel), whose name-saint dictated the Matthew subject matter.
w: Drop a coin in the light box and three Caravaggios flare from the dark in the Contarelli Chapel: the Calling, the Inspiration and the Martyrdom, painted 1599-1602. The Calling steals the room -- a shaft of raking light, and Christ's pointing hand summoning a tax collector from his coins. The French national church holds them.

n: Basilica di Sant'Agostino in Campo Marzio
hook: Caravaggio's barefoot, dirt-soled pilgrims kneel before the Madonna in a church a stone's throw from the Pantheon.
facts: Holds Caravaggio's Madonna di Loreto (Madonna dei Pellegrini), painted around 1604-1606, in the first chapel on the left.
The painting caused controversy for showing pilgrims with visibly dirty bare feet kneeling before the Virgin.
Raphael frescoed the Prophet Isaiah (1512) on a pier of the left nave, part of a monument for Johann Goritz.
Jacopo Sansovino's marble Madonna del Parto (1521), patroness of childbirth, stands near the entrance and is still venerated by expectant mothers.
The church sits in the rione Sant'Eustachio, with its parish offices on Via della Scrofa.
legend: The Madonna del Parto is locally credited with answering prayers for safe childbirth, and is traditionally surrounded by ex-voto offerings.
w: A short walk from the Pantheon, Caravaggio's barefoot pilgrims kneel before the Virgin, dirty soles turned to the viewer -- a scandal when it was unveiled around 1605. Raphael frescoed an Isaiah on a nave pier; Sansovino carved a Madonna del Parto that expectant mothers still pray to, amid ex-votos. Three masters in one quiet church.

n: Sant'Ignazio di Loyola (the painted fake dome)
hook: Stand on the marble disc in the floor and a soaring dome opens overhead - except the ceiling is flat and the dome is paint.
facts: Andrea Pozzo, a Jesuit lay brother, painted the church's illusionistic ceiling and its famous trompe-l'oeil 'dome' from around 1685.
A real dome was never built (reportedly for lack of funds / objections); Pozzo instead painted a flat canvas that simulates a coffered cupola.
A marble disc set into the nave floor marks the single viewing point where the perspective resolves; step off it and the dome visibly distorts.
The nave vault fresco depicts the Apotheosis (Glory) of Saint Ignatius, with allegories of the four continents.
The painted 'dome' on view today is a 19th-century recreation (by Francesco Manno, 1823) after the original was damaged.
w: Stand on the marble disc in the floor and a vast dome soars overhead -- step off it, and the dome buckles and warps, because the ceiling is flat and the dome is paint. Andrea Pozzo, a Jesuit lay brother, brushed the illusion around 1685 when the cupola ran out of money. Above the nave, his Saint Ignatius ascends through trompe-l'oeil sky.

hook: Four sunken Republican temples where Julius Caesar was cut down - and where Rome's stray cats now hold court.
facts: The Area Sacra holds four Republican-era temples (labelled A-D), the oldest dating to the early 3rd century BC, excavated under Mussolini from 1929.
The site adjoins the remains of the Curia of Pompey, the hall where Julius Caesar was assassinated on 15 March 44 BC.
Temple B is a distinctive circular temple to Fortuna Huiusce Diei (101 BC); Temple A is associated with Juturna.
The Torre Argentina Cat Sanctuary, founded in 1993, cares for the colony of stray cats living among the ruins.
Walkways and a public entrance opened in 2023, letting visitors descend into the site for the first time.
legend: Popular tradition pinpoints the exact spot of Caesar's murder within the square, though the precise location within the Curia is debated.
w: Four sunken Republican temples, the oldest from the early 3rd century BC, dug out below the modern street -- and beside them the hall where Julius Caesar was cut down on the Ides of March, 44 BC. Since 1993 a cat sanctuary has run among the ruins, the colony dozing on ancient stone. Walkways finally let visitors down in 2023.

hook: Piazza Navona's elongated oval is no accident - it sits on the running track of a vanished Roman stadium, still visible five metres down.
facts: The stadium was built under Emperor Domitian and inaugurated around 85-86 AD as Rome's first permanent masonry stadium for Greek-style athletics.
It measured roughly 265-276 m long by about 106 m wide and held an estimated 30,000 spectators.
Piazza Navona preserves the stadium's footprint: the square's distinctive elongated shape traces the ancient arena.
Excavated remains of the north (curved) end are open as an underground archaeological museum about 4-5 m below the modern square.
The museum entrance is on Via di Tor Sanguigna, just off the north end of Piazza Navona.
legend: The name 'Navona' is popularly traced to 'in agone' (the games), the term for the stadium's athletic contests, slurred over centuries into n'agone / navona.
w: Piazza Navona's long oval is no accident: it traces the running track of Domitian's stadium, raised around 85 AD for Greek athletics and seating thirty thousand. The curved north end survives five metres down, an underground museum of the arena the square was built on top of. Even the name 'Navona' may slur from 'in agone', the games.

hook: The mother church of the Jesuits, where a daily 'baroque machine' slides a painting away to reveal St Ignatius beneath Baciccio's ceiling that boils over into the nave.
facts: The Gesù is the mother church of the Society of Jesus and the burial place of its founder, Saint Ignatius of Loyola.
Giovanni Battista Gaulli (Baciccio) frescoed the nave vault with the Triumph of the Name of Jesus, completed around 1678-1679, with figures spilling illusionistically beyond the gilt frame.
Andrea Pozzo designed the sumptuous altar-tomb of Saint Ignatius (1696-1700), encrusted with lapis lazuli and gilt bronze.
Each day (traditionally at 17:30) a 'macchina barocca' lowers a large painting to reveal the statue of Saint Ignatius, with lighting and music.
The original silver statue of Saint Ignatius was melted down under Pope Pius VI to help pay reparations to Napoleon (Treaty of Tolentino, 1797); the present figure is a copy.
w: The mother church of the Jesuits, where Baciccio's ceiling boils over its gilt frame and spills figures into the nave. Each evening a 'baroque machine' lowers a painting to reveal St Ignatius beneath, with lights and music. His altar drips lapis and bronze; the original silver saint was melted to pay Napoleon. Theatre staged as faith.

hook: Peer through a small bronze keyhole in a green door and St Peter's dome appears perfectly framed at the end of a hedge-lined avenue, miles across the city.
facts: The keyhole is in the gate of the Priory of the Knights of Malta (Villa del Priorato di Malta) at Piazza dei Cavalieri di Malta 3 on the Aventine.
The piazza and the priory's facade were designed by the engraver-architect Giovanni Battista Piranesi in 1764-66; it was his only completed architectural work.
Through the keyhole the dome of St Peter's Basilica is seen framed by a clipped hedge avenue in the priory garden.
The Sovereign Military Order of Malta is a sovereign entity, so the view is sometimes described as looking across three 'states' — the Order, Italy and the Vatican.
legend: Popular accounts claim the alignment with St Peter's was a deliberate optical trick by Piranesi; the precise garden layout that produces the view is later, so the famous framing is at least partly a happy accident.
w: Put your eye to a small bronze keyhole in a green door on the Aventine, and St Peter's dome appears framed at the end of a clipped hedge avenue, a mile across the city. Piranesi designed the priory gate of the Knights of Malta in the 1760s -- his only built work. The famous alignment, the story goes, was as much luck as design.

hook: A walled garden of cypresses and roaming cats beneath the Pyramid of Cestius where Keats lies under a stone reading 'Here lies one whose name was writ in water.'
facts: The non-Catholic cemetery (Cimitero Acattolico) lies against the Aurelian Walls beside the Pyramid of Cestius in Testaccio; the earliest known burial dates to 1716.
John Keats (d. 1821) is buried here under an epitaph he chose: 'Here lies one whose name was writ in water'; his friend the painter Joseph Severn lies beside him.
Percy Bysshe Shelley's ashes were interred here after he drowned in 1822; his stone is inscribed 'Cor Cordium' (heart of hearts) with lines from Shakespeare's Tempest.
The Italian Marxist Antonio Gramsci (d. 1937) and former Italian president Giorgio Napolitano (d. 2023) are also buried here.
A colony of cats is cared for among the graves; the cemetery is run by a private non-profit and survives partly on donations.
w: A walled garden of cypresses and dozing cats under the Pyramid of Cestius, where Rome buried those its churches would not. Keats lies beneath a stone he chose -- 'Here lies one whose name was writ in water' -- Severn beside him. Shelley's ashes rest nearby under 'Cor Cordium'. Gramsci too. A quiet corner for poets and outsiders.

hook: A 37-metre marble-clad pyramid wedged into Rome's ancient walls — the tomb of a magistrate seized by Egyptomania a generation before the pharaohs became fashionable.
facts: Built c. 18-12 BC as the tomb of Gaius Cestius, a magistrate and member of the Epulones priestly college; an inscription records it was completed in 330 days.
It is brick-faced concrete clad in slabs of white Carrara-type marble on a travertine foundation, about 29.6 m square and 37 m (125 Roman feet) high.
It was incorporated into the Aurelian Walls (271-275 AD), which is why it survives so well.
The sealed burial chamber, frescoed in the Third Pompeian style, was rediscovered and opened in 1660 under Pope Alexander VII.
legend: Medieval Romans believed it was the 'tomb of Remus' (with the Vatican-area pyramid the 'tomb of Romulus'), a misattribution corrected only when the inscriptions were read in the Renaissance.
w: A 37-metre marble-clad pyramid jammed into Rome's ancient walls -- the tomb of a magistrate gripped by Egyptomania a generation before Cleopatra. An inscription brags: 330 days. The Aurelian Walls swallowed it in the 270s AD, which is why it stands sharp; medieval Romans thought it Remus's tomb. A pharaoh's geometry on the Tiber.

hook: A 35-metre 'hill' that is entirely artificial — a tidy ancient landfill of some 53 million smashed olive-oil jars, now honeycombed with cellars and nightclubs.
facts: The mound is composed almost entirely of fragments of broken amphorae — an estimated 53 million vessels — making it one of the largest known ancient waste sites.
It stands about 35 m high with a circumference of nearly a kilometre; deposition dates mainly to c. AD 140-250.
The amphorae were mostly Dressel 20 jars that carried olive oil from Baetica (southern Spain); the oil-soaked sherds could not be recycled into concrete, so they were stacked here.
The hill's cool, porous interior was later quarried for cellars; today bars and clubs (the 'Monte de' cocci' nightlife) occupy caves cut into its flanks.
w: A 35-metre hill that is wholly man-made -- an ancient rubbish tip of some 53 million smashed olive-oil jars, stacked into a landmark. The oil-soaked shards could not be reused, so Rome piled them here from the 2nd century. Cool caves later cut into its flanks now hold cellars and nightclubs. The city's trash, grown into a mountain.

hook: Descend through three Romes in one building: a 12th-century basilica, a 4th-century church below it, and at the bottom a 2nd-century Mithraic temple beside running water.
facts: The upper basilica was rebuilt under Cardinal Anastasius c. 1099-1123 and is famous for its 12th-century apse mosaic of the Cross as the Tree of Life.
Beneath it lies a 4th-century basilica with early-medieval frescoes; the lower levels were rediscovered from the 1860s by the Irish Dominican prior Joseph Mullooly.
At the lowest level is a 2nd-century mithraeum (sanctuary of Mithras), its cult room about 9.6 m by 6 m, built within a Roman apartment block.
An underground stream still runs audibly through the lowest level; a drainage tunnel built in 1912 manages the persistent groundwater.
The church and excavations are owned and run by the Irish Dominicans.
w: Descend through three Romes stacked in one building: a 12th-century basilica with a gold Tree-of-Life mosaic, a 4th-century church below it, and at the bottom a 2nd-century temple of Mithras, beside an underground stream still heard running. Irish Dominicans dug it out from the 1860s and own it yet. A core sample of the eternal city.

hook: Rome's oldest round church, ringed by a 16th-century fresco cycle of martyrdoms so graphic that Dickens called the gallery a chamber of horrors.
facts: Built in the 5th century under Pope Leo I and consecrated by Pope Simplicius (468-483), it is the earliest centrally planned (circular) church in Rome.
Its inner wall carries 34 frescoes of martyrdom scenes painted in the 1580s by Niccolo Circignani (Pomarancio) and Antonio Tempesta for Pope Gregory XIII, with explanatory captions.
Charles Dickens, in Pictures from Italy (1846), recoiled at the 'representations of the bodily torments of saints and martyrs' as the most hideous he had ever seen.
It has served as the Hungarian national church in Rome since the late 18th century, honouring St Stephen of Hungary.
A 2nd-century mithraeum linked to the Castra Peregrinorum barracks lies beneath the church (not open to the public).
w: Rome's oldest round church, a 5th-century ring of columns -- and around its wall, 34 frescoes of martyrdoms so lurid that Dickens called them the most hideous things he had ever seen. Pomarancio painted the flayings and burnings in the 1580s, each captioned in detail. The Hungarian national church now, beautiful and appalling at once.

hook: A triumphal marble waterfall on the Janiculum, built of plundered ancient stone and immortalised as the opening shot of The Great Beauty.
facts: Completed 1610-1614 for Pope Paul V (Borghese) as the show-fountain ending the restored Acqua Paola / Aqua Traiana aqueduct; architects Giovanni Fontana and Flaminio Ponzio.
It reuses ancient marble spoils from the Roman Forum and the Temple of Minerva, and granite columns taken from the facade of Old St Peter's Basilica.
In 1690 Carlo Fontana enlarged it under Pope Alexander VIII, replacing the original five basins with one huge semicircular pool.
It opens Paolo Sorrentino's Oscar-winning film La Grande Bellezza (2013) and overlooks a sweeping panorama of Rome.
w: A triumphal marble waterfall on the Janiculum, built for Pope Paul V in 1612 from looted antiquity -- marble off the Forum, granite columns stripped from the front of Old St Peter's. One huge basin replaced the original five in 1690. It opens 'The Great Beauty', and behind it Rome spreads out below. Plunder, turned to spectacle.

hook: A perfect miniature circular temple hidden in a courtyard on the Janiculum — Bramante's manifesto of the High Renaissance, marking the supposed spot of St Peter's crucifixion.
facts: The Tempietto stands in the cloister courtyard of San Pietro in Montorio on the Janiculum slope; it was designed by Donato Bramante, possibly built as early as 1502.
It marks the traditional site believed to be where St Peter was crucified.
It was commissioned by Ferdinand and Isabella of Spain and is considered a foundational masterpiece of High Renaissance architecture, a circular peripteral 'temple' of Doric columns.
Bramante's domed, centrally planned design influenced his later plans for the new St Peter's Basilica.
legend: The dedication rests on the tradition that Peter was martyred here on the Janiculum, though most scholars place his crucifixion across the river near the Vatican.
w: A perfect little circular temple hidden in a courtyard on the Janiculum -- Bramante's manifesto of the High Renaissance, raised around 1502 on the spot tradition marks as St Peter's crucifixion. Sixteen Doric columns ring a tiny domed cell. Ferdinand and Isabella paid for it; it shaped Bramante's dreams for St Peter's itself.

hook: A fairy-tale pocket of fused Liberty, Baroque, medieval and Art Nouveau fantasy, hung around a frog fountain and a wrought-iron chandelier arch.
facts: An ensemble of 18 palazzi and 27 buildings and villini designed by Florentine architect Gino Coppedè, built 1915-1927.
Coppedè died in 1927 before completion; the work was finished by Paolo Emilio André.
Its heart is Piazza Mincio, with the Fontana delle Rane (Fountain of the Frogs), built in 1924.
The Palazzo del Ragno on the piazza is named for the large spider carved over its doorway, an allusion to human labour.
The main entrance from Via Tagliamento is a great arch hung with an enormous wrought-iron chandelier.
The district straddles the boundary of the Trieste and Salario quarters, between Via Salaria and Via Nomentana.
legend: Local lore claims the Beatles dipped in the Fontana delle Rane fully clothed after a 1965 concert at the nearby Piper Club, though the story is unverified.
w: Step under a great arch hung with a wrought-iron chandelier and into a fairy-tale: 1920s palazzi that fuse Liberty, Baroque, medieval and Art Nouveau into one fever dream around a frog fountain. Gino Coppede built 27 of them before he died in 1927. Local lore swears the Beatles splashed in the fountain -- unproven.

hook: A whimsical Liberty-style cottage of turrets, majolica and owl-themed stained glass, hidden in the park of Mussolini's old residence.
facts: Built from an 1840 'Swiss hut' by Giuseppe Jappelli for the Torlonia family; transformed into a medieval-village fantasy from 1908 by Enrico Gennari and into Liberty style by Vincenzo Fasolo from 1917.
It takes its name (House of the Little Owls) from the recurring owl motif in its decoration.
The house is renowned for Italian Liberty stained glass made 1908-1930 by Duilio Cambellotti, Umberto Bottazzi, Vittorio Grassi and Paolo Paschetto.
It was the residence of Prince Giovanni Torlonia Jr. until his death in 1938; today it is a municipal museum of stained glass.

It sits in Villa Torlonia, which Mussolini rented from the Torlonia for one lira a year and used as his state residence from the mid-1920s until 1943; an airtight bunker was built beneath the main villa.
Address: Via Nomentana 70, in the Nomentano district.
w: A whimsical Liberty cottage of turrets, majolica and owl-themed stained glass, tucked in the park of the villa Mussolini rented for one lira a year. It grew from an 1840 hut into this fantasy by 1917; Cambellotti filled it with glowing glass. The little owls give it its name. Now a museum of stained glass, in Il Duce's old garden.

hook: White marble gods of ancient Rome posed against the black diesel engines and turbines of the city's first power station.
facts: Housed in Rome's first public electricity plant, the Giovanni Montemartini thermoelectric station, which generated power from the 1890s to the 1930s.

Classical sculpture from the Capitoline Museums was placed here temporarily in 1997 during renovations; the display 'Le macchine e gli dei' (The Machines and the Gods) proved so popular it became permanent.
Around 400 ancient sculptures, plus mosaics and inscriptions, are shown among preserved boilers, turbines and two large 1930s diesel engines.
Many works were excavated from Rome's ancient horti (patrician gardens) in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
Address: Via Ostiense 106, in the industrial Ostiense district near Garbatella metro.
w: White marble gods posed against the black turbines and diesel engines of the city's first power station. The Capitoline parked its overflow of statues here in 1997 during a refit; the clash of cool antiquity and industrial iron proved so striking it stayed. Some 400 sculptures among the boilers. The machines and the gods, sharing a hall.

hook: The 'Square Colosseum', a stark grid of arches built to glorify the Fascist regime and now Fendi's headquarters.
facts: Designed in the late 1930s by Giovanni Guerrini, Ernesto Bruno La Padula and Mario Romano for the planned 1942 World's Fair (E42), which Mussolini's regime never held due to WWII.
Its travertine facade has six rows of nine arches; the nine columns are said to echo the letters in 'Benito' and 'Mussolini'.
It is nicknamed the 'Colosseo Quadrato' (Square Colosseum) for its arched, Colosseum-like silhouette.
An inscription on the facade quotes Mussolini, hailing Italians as 'a people of poets, artists, heroes, saints, thinkers, scientists, navigators and migrants'.
Since 2015 the fashion house Fendi has leased the building as its headquarters, with a public exhibition space on the ground floor.
It stands in the EUR district, a planned quarter in Rome's south.
w: The 'Square Colosseum' -- a stark travertine block of six rows of nine arches, built for Mussolini's regime for a 1942 World's Fair that war cancelled. The nine arches per row are said to count the letters of 'Benito Mussolini'. A Fascist boast still runs across its face: a 'people of poets, heroes, navigators'. Fendi works inside it now.

hook: A private villa on Rome's highest point holding the only ceiling painting Caravaggio ever made, and once put up for auction at €471 million.
facts: The casino was built around 1570 on the Pincian (the highest point in central Rome) and has belonged to the Boncompagni Ludovisi family since 1621.

A small upstairs chamber holds Caravaggio's 'Jupiter, Neptune and Pluto' (c. 1597), an oil-on-plaster ceiling and his only known ceiling work, painted for Cardinal Francesco Maria del Monte's alchemy room.
The Caravaggio ceiling had been covered over and was rediscovered only in 1969 by Giuliana Zandri.
The villa is named for Guercino's 1621 ceiling fresco 'Aurora' in the main hall, painted as a riposte to Guido Reni's 'Aurora' nearby.
After an inheritance dispute it was put to auction in January 2022 with an opening bid of about €471 million and received no offers; later auctions cut the price.
It is entered from Via Lombardia in the Ludovisi district, off Via Veneto.
w: A private villa on the Pincian with the only ceiling Caravaggio ever painted -- Jupiter, Neptune and Pluto, brushed onto plaster around 1597 for a cardinal's alchemy room, lost under later coats until 1969. The hall below carries Guercino's 'Aurora'. Put to auction in 2022 at a record price near half a billion, it drew not a single bid.

hook: Rome's vast monumental cemetery, a city of statuary and tombs beside the basilica that Allied bombs gutted in 1943.
facts: Covering roughly 83 hectares, it is Rome's main monumental cemetery, named after the Verani family who once owned the land.
Begun under the Napoleonic administration (initial designs by Giuseppe Valadier, 1807-1812) beside the early Christian catacombs at San Lorenzo.
Architect Virginio Vespignani built the monumental entrance gate and the cemetery church of Santa Maria della Misericordia (consecrated 1860) under Popes Gregory XVI and Pius IX.
It holds the tombs of figures including poet Trilussa and actors of Italian cinema, amid elaborate 19th- and 20th-century funerary sculpture.
Its monumental entrance and Pincetto area were damaged in the Allied bombing of the San Lorenzo district on 19 July 1943.
It lies on Piazzale del Verano in the San Lorenzo / Tiburtino quarter, next to the Basilica di San Lorenzo fuori le mura.
w: Rome's vast monumental cemetery, a city of statuary and chapels begun under Napoleon beside the catacombs of San Lorenzo. Trilussa and the stars of Italian cinema lie among its 19th-century tombs. The grand entrance was scarred when Allied bombs gutted the San Lorenzo district in July 1943. Eighty-three hectares of marble grief.

hook: A massive cylindrical Roman tomb on the Appian Way, later swallowed into a medieval castle that levied tolls on travellers.
facts: A drum-shaped mausoleum built c. 30-20 BC at the third mile of the Via Appia for Caecilia Metella, daughter of consul Quintus Caecilius Metellus.
The travertine-faced drum is crowned by a marble frieze of garlands and ox skulls (bucrania), which gave the spot the name 'Capo di Bove'.
Around 1300 the Caetani family, kin of Pope Boniface VIII, fortified it with battlements (added by 1302) and incorporated it into the Castrum Caetani.
From the castle the Caetani controlled and taxed traffic along the Appian Way.
It stands within the Parco Archeologico dell'Appia Antica, opposite the brick complex of the medieval castle and church of San Nicola.
w: A massive cylindrical tomb on the Appian Way, raised around 20 BC for a consul's daughter and ringed with a marble frieze of ox skulls -- which named the spot Capo di Bove. Around 1300 the Caetani, kin of a pope, battlemented it into a castle and taxed everyone who passed. Two thousand years standing guard over the old road south.

hook: A green plain where the colossal arches of ancient Roman aqueducts march across the grass, a favourite of filmmakers.
facts: A 240-hectare public park in southeast Rome, part of the Appian Way Regional Park, between Via Appia and Via Tuscolana.
It is named for the remains of seven aqueducts that crossed here, including the surviving arches of the 1st-century AD Aqua Claudia.
The Renaissance-era Acqua Felice aqueduct (1585-1587) also runs through the park.
Its sweeping arches have featured in films, notably the opening of Fellini's 'La Dolce Vita' and scenes in Sorrentino's 'La Grande Bellezza'.
The park lies in the Appio Claudio area, reachable from the Giulio Agricola and Subaugusta metro stations.
w: A green plain on Rome's edge where the arches of ancient aqueducts stride across the grass -- the 1st-century Aqua Claudia chief among seven that crossed here. The Renaissance Acqua Felice threads through too. Fellini opened 'La Dolce Vita' among these arches, and Sorrentino has filmed here since. Empire-scale plumbing, gone to parkland.
